channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: 'Text is evil:' How making Peak changed indie studio Aggro Crab
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

March 19, 2026

4 Min Read

Image via Landfall Games / Aggro Crab

Peak was famously made in one month. Well, actually, it was made in around four months, but the core experience was largely cobbled together in around four weeks during a meeting of the minds in South Korea between indie studios Aggro Crab and Landfall Games.

The social climber went on to sell million of copies and became the very definition of 'friendslop.' According to Aggro Crab studio head, Nick Kaman, it also changed the indie studio for the better.

During a talk at GDC Festival of Gaming, Kaman outlined how building Peak with a focus on rapid iteration and fluid ideation resulted in him rethinking how he wanted Aggro Crab to operate. His influence? Co-developer Landfall Games, who he described as something of a "cowboy" studio that played fast and loose during production in order to properly lock in. 

"Landfall are deeply evil people," joked Kaman. "[...] They have zero tolerance for friction as a studio, and that's where their superpower is. They won't use task-tracking software. Our compromise was a Discord channel where you emoji the task that's complete. 

Related:Direct-join union UVW-CWA now has 550 members

"They wouldn't have meetings with me. I would come down from my room to the office and be like 'hey guys, want to do a quick huddle before we start today?' And they'd tell me to fuck off; Like, 'Nick, I know what I'm doing. Why do we need a meeting?"

Kaman also noted that Landfall CEO and designer Wilhelm Nylund would only type the letter 'f' when posting git commits, because it is clearly the "fastest key." 

"This is what we were dealing with. This is called cowboy development. Look, I thought I was the cowboy. I thought I was this rootin' tootin' indie developer who didn't play by the rules, who took shortcuts and made stuff fast," added Kaman. "But when I saw them I realised I'm like a NASA engineer compared to them." 

Kaman described that approach as fun, fast, and wild—but noted there are some consequences to moving at breakneck speed. Case in point: He said Peak is basically written in "spaghetti code," and as a result is pretty hard to update. "It's full of bugs and players tell us about it every day," he added. 

Even so, Kaman said the upsides outweigh the downsides, and noted that he took a few cues from Landfall after working on Peak. For instance, Aggro Crab is now less reliant on text chat and more committed to talking through issues on impromptu calls or in-person. That change, he said, came after Aggro Crab and Landfall found themselves in frequent conflict when trying to nail down the concept for Peak over Discord (before they relocated to South Korea). 

Related:Unionized game workers launch petition to protect immigrants from ICE agents

"We came up with this phrase: text is evil. We realized the Discord vibes must have been caused by some sort of technology in Discord where I send a message and then Discord warps it into the most negative version of how that message can be received, and then it comes out the other end. Someone has to fix that," he said. "But in person, all of the nuance and all of the energy is there when you're talking to people. So ideas flow more naturally, arguments get resolved quicker, and you have a better time." 

"Freedom of development feels so good"

In the longer term, Kaman explained Peak's meteoric success made him realize Aggro Crab had suffered because of how they were working. 

"We were working too hard on things that might not matter that much in very rigid pipelines, so we changed a lot. First off, we added spontaneous communication everywhere," said Kaman.

We have an office in Seattle. We have free lunches on Wednesdays now to just encourage people to come in. It's still hybrid. You can work from home every other day of the week. You can work from home on Wednesday, but if you're if you come in, we can go to lunch together and maybe talk about games or talk about stuff besides games.

Related:Lightspeed Studios unveils proprietary development framework to build titles 'rooted in reality'

"We moved the desks at the office so I could just turn around and talk to someone and show them my screen. If I'm in the office, that's the expectation. You know, that's less friction. We have less scheduled meetings. No one likes scheduled meetings. Why do we do so many? We have impromptu meetings and more opportunities to work independently." 

Kaman said he realized he was also something of a dictator. "I was nice, but I was everywhere on Another Crab's Treasure," he admitted. As a result, he has now made a concerted effort to place more trust in the team—which, he notes, they have absolutely earned—and become more hands off. 

Aggro Crab has also implemented a new rule that allows employees to spend every other Friday working on whatever they want. Something that isn't related to the studio's current project. 

"Peak is the most fun I've ever had making a game, and I think that did more for me than the success of the game," said Kaman. 

"It did save us financially and mentally, but it led me to this realization of 'why aren't we making more games like this?' Freedom of development feels so good, and gets such good results." 

Game Developer and GDC Festival of Gaming are sibling organizations under Informa Festivals. 

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/production/-text-is-evil-how-making-peak-changed-indie-studio-aggro-crab
